# BannermanLeasing / Bannerman Content Machine
### Plain-English Explanation for a Non-Technical Person

---

## What Is This?

This is a **custom website the owner built to automatically write social media posts and blog articles for their property leasing business — Bannerman Leasing.**

Here's the problem it was trying to solve: a small leasing company needs to post consistently on Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, Twitter, and a blog to attract landlords and tenants. Hiring someone to write all that content is expensive. Writing it yourself takes months. So the owner built a tool that uses artificial intelligence — the same kind of technology behind ChatGPT — to generate an entire year's worth of content at once, already tailored to the leasing business.

Think of it like a very smart content calendar assistant. You tell it the topics you care about (things like "tips for first-time renters" or "how to screen tenants"), and it writes a full post for every platform, for every week of the year, automatically. In theory, you could set it up once and have fresh, on-brand content ready to copy and post every single day without hiring anyone.

**The bottom line:** It's a privately built content automation tool for a single small business. It is not a product being sold to other people. No strangers are using it. It was likely built to save money on marketing.

---

## How Was It Built and Deployed? (Plain English)

The tool is a website that lives on the internet and talks to several outside services to do its job. Here's what each piece does:

**The website itself** is hosted on a service called **Cloudflare Pages** — think of this like the landlord who provides the building where the website lives. Cloudflare has a free tier, so this likely costs nothing or very little per month.

**The artificial intelligence** that actually writes the content comes from one of three AI companies: **Anthropic** (makers of Claude), **OpenAI** (makers of ChatGPT), or **Google** (makers of Gemini). The owner pays these companies a small fee each time the tool generates content — roughly a few cents per piece of writing. A full year's worth of content generated all at once would cost somewhere between $5 and $20 total.

**The content schedule and brand voice** — things like "always sound professional but approachable" and "week 12 is about pet-friendly rentals" — are written directly into the tool's code. They are specific to Bannerman Leasing. If someone else wanted to use this tool for a different business, they'd have to rewrite those parts.

**A database service called Supabase** (think of it like a filing cabinet that lives on the internet) was set up to store the content calendar so the tool could run automatically in the background each day without anyone logging in. However — and this is important — **this automatic daily feature was never fully finished** (more on that below).

**Everything is stored in a system called GitHub**, which is like a version-controlled filing cabinet for the code itself. Think of it as Google Docs for software — it tracks every change ever made.

---

## What's Working and What Isn't?

### What Works:
- You can open the website, load up the year's content plan, and click a button to generate social media posts and blog articles
- The AI writing works correctly and produces content for all the platforms (blog, Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, Twitter)
- The website is stable and can be deployed to the internet

### What Doesn't Work (or Is Broken):

**The biggest problem: Everything disappears when you close the browser tab.** The tool saves your work in something called "session storage," which is like a whiteboard — the moment you close the tab or the browser, everything is erased. There is no "save" feature for normal everyday use. This means every time someone uses it, they have to regenerate everything from scratch.

**The automatic daily feature is half-built.** The owner's vision was that the tool would automatically generate the next day's content every morning without anyone having to log in — like a coffee maker on a timer. The machinery for this was built, but the "timer" part was never added. So the automatic feature simply never runs. Someone would have to trigger it manually.

**Security is off by default.** The tool has no password or login system unless the owner specifically turned one on. It was designed to be used privately, but if someone found the web address, they could potentially use it and run up the owner's AI bill.

**The Windows-computer problem.** Part of the process for publishing updates to the website uses a command that only works on Mac or Linux computers. Anyone trying to update this from a regular Windows PC would run into an error.

---

## Is This Worth Anything? What Should Happen to It?

**Honest assessment: It has modest value, but not as a ready-to-sell product.**

The tool is smart and well-built for what it is, but it has some significant unfinished pieces (the disappearing content problem, the broken automation), and it is deeply tied to Bannerman Leasing specifically — the entire personality, tone, and topic schedule is written for that one business.

Here's what it could realistically be worth:

- **To another leasing or property management company:** Low value without significant customization work. They'd essentially be buying raw material, not a finished product.
- **To a software developer or small agency:** Moderate value — perhaps **$500 to $2,500** — as a starting template they could customize and resell to other small businesses. The hard architectural work (connecting to multiple AI services, the calendar system, the database structure) is already done.
- **As a product sold to many customers:** Not ready. It has no billing system, no accounts, no way to support multiple businesses at once.

**Recommended path:** Either sell it as a developer template on a marketplace (like a platform called Gumroad, which is similar to Etsy but for digital products), or simply make it freely available online to generate goodwill. Trying to operate it as a business would require significant additional development work — probably several weeks from a developer.

**If you simply do nothing:** The website will eventually go offline when the Cloudflare account expires or the AI service subscriptions lapse. The code itself (in GitHub) would remain intact indefinitely as long as that account stays open.

---

## Access and Accounts (What You Need to Log In To)

To manage or transfer this tool, you will need to locate the usernames and passwords for the following services. Check the owner's password manager, email inbox, or browser saved passwords:

| Service | What It Is | What Happens If You Stop Paying / Lose Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloudflare** (cloudflare.com) | Where the website lives | Website goes offline |
| **GitHub** (github.com) | Where all the code is stored | Code becomes inaccessible; nothing breaks immediately but you lose the source |
| **Anthropic / OpenAI / Google AI** | The AI that writes the content | Content generation stops working immediately |
| **Supabase** (supabase.com) | The database that was meant to store the content calendar | The automatic daily feature (already not working) stops entirely |

**Important notes:**
- The AI services (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) charge **per use**, like a utility bill — not a flat monthly fee. If no one is using the tool, the bill is essentially zero.
- Cloudflare and Supabase both have **free tiers** that this tool almost certainly fits within, so there may be no monthly cost at all for those services.
- The secret "keys" that let the tool talk to the AI services are stored in the Cloudflare settings dashboard, not in the code files themselves. You would need Cloudflare account access to find or change them.

---

## Why the Owner Built This (Personal Context)

This tool appears to have been built by the owner **for their own business**, not as something they were selling to others. It reflects a common situation for small business owners who have technical skills: rather than paying a marketing agency potentially thousands of dollars per month to produce social media content, they built their own system.

The choice to use multiple AI providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google) suggests the owner was experimenting to find the best quality or price. The level of detail in the content schedule — 52 weeks of specific leasing topics, a defined brand voice — suggests real time and thought went into this.

The fact that it was never fully finished (the automatic daily feature, the save problem) is very common in personal projects built by business owners. The urgent business problems get solved first, and the polish never quite arrives.

---

## What Should I Do? (Action Steps)

**1. Secure the accounts before anything else.**
Log into or locate the passwords for Cloudflare, GitHub, Anthropic/OpenAI/Google, and Supabase. Transfer these accounts to a new owner or administrator, or at minimum make sure you have access to them. Start with Cloudflare — that's where the live website and most of the sensitive keys live. If you can't find login information, check the owner's email for account confirmation messages from these services.

**2. Check whether any of these services are actively billing.**
Log into each account and look for billing or subscription information. The AI services (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) charge only when the tool is used — if no one is actively using it, the charges should be near zero. Cloudflare and Supabase are very likely on free plans. If you find any active billing, decide whether to cancel or continue based on whether you plan to keep the tool running.

**3. Decide on the tool's fate within 30 days.**
You have three realistic options: (A) **Keep it running** as-is for Bannerman Leasing's ongoing use — it works for generating content manually even with its flaws; (B) **Hire a developer to fix it and either sell it or open-source it** — budget roughly $1,000–$3,000 for a developer to fix the known problems and package it for sale; or (C) **Archive the code and shut it down** — download the code from GitHub as a ZIP file for your records, then let the services lapse. Option C costs you nothing and preserves the work.

**4. Do not delete the GitHub repository without downloading a copy first.**
Even if you decide to shut everything down, the code in GitHub represents real intellectual work. Before closing or abandoning any accounts, go to GitHub, find the BannermanLeasing repository, and download a copy as a ZIP file (there is a button for this — no technical skill required). Store it somewhere safe like Google Drive. This costs nothing and ensures the work is not lost.

**5. If you plan to sell the business or this tool, get a developer's opinion first.**
Before accepting any offer or setting any price, pay a developer for one to two hours of their time to review the code and give you an honest valuation. The range of $500–$2,500 in this document is an informed estimate, but a developer who specializes in these kinds of tools would give you a more precise number and could help you list it on the right marketplace. Do not let anyone tell you it is worth nothing — the underlying work is real — but also do not expect it to sell for tens of thousands of dollars without significant additional development.
